draft.introduction: This excerpt from the Jain Agamic literature presents the doctrine of anekantavada (many-sidedness), the distinctive Jain epistemological principle that reality is multifaceted and no single perspective can capture the whole truth.

translation.text: The wise ones of old have said: everything exists from one standpoint and does not exist from another. It is permanent from one standpoint and impermanent from another. The soul is one thing from its own perspective and another from the perspective of its changing states. Those who see only one side of things and proclaim it as the whole truth are like blind men touching an elephant—each one describes a different animal.

translator_notes.note: The parable of the blind men and the elephant, though widely attributed to Buddhist sources, appears in Jain literature as well and perfectly illustrates anekantavada. This doctrine is not mere relativism: it holds that all perspectives contain partial truth and that reality itself is genuinely complex. It provided the philosophical basis for the remarkable Jain tradition of intellectual tolerance.
